# Fusion Framework Server — Full Reference for LLM Assistants

This document is the authoritative reference when writing or modifying
code that uses `@fusion-framework/server`. It supersedes the README for
anything the two disagree on. If something is ambiguous here, prefer
reading the source files linked from `llms.txt`.

---

## 1. Mental model

Fusion is a thin dispatcher on top of AWS Lambda. One Lambda function
receives all API Gateway events (and optionally non-HTTP events), and
Fusion dispatches them to the right controller method or listener based
on decorator metadata.

Runtime flow for an HTTP request:

1. API Gateway invokes the Lambda with `event.httpMethod` and
   `event.resource` (the template path, e.g. `/users/{id}`).
2. `FusionServer.handleController(event)` looks up
   `this[event.httpMethod][event.resource]` → a string encoding the
   target controller and method.
3. The server resolves the controller class from tsyringe and calls
   the method with `event` as the only positional arg.
4. The return value is wrapped: a `FusionResponse` gets `toResponse()`
   applied; `undefined`/`null` becomes 204 No Content; anything else is
   JSON-stringified with status 200 and `Content-Type: application/json`.

Listener (non-HTTP) flow: the event is matched by event-name first
(`evt.event` key), otherwise by the first pattern config that matches.

---

## 2. Path parameter syntax — `{id}` only

**This is the most common mistake LLMs make.** Fusion matches routes
against `event.resource`, which API Gateway fills with the *template*
path. API Gateway uses `{id}` notation in its templates. Therefore:

```ts
// ✅ Correct
@Get('/users/{id}')
@Put('/orders/{orderId}/items/{itemId}')
@Delete('/tenants/{tenantId}/users/{userId}')

// ❌ Wrong — will NOT match
@Get('/users/:id')
@Put('/orders/:orderId/items/:itemId')
```

To read path parameter values inside the handler, use
`event.pathParameters?.id` — those keys match the names inside `{...}`.

Do not file feature requests to add `:id` support. The framework
deliberately enforces a single syntax to keep projects consistent.

---

## 3. Controller basics

```ts
import { Controller, Get, Post, FusionResponse } from '@fusion-framework/server';
import { APIGatewayEvent } from 'aws-lambda';

@Controller('/users')
export class UsersController {
  @Get('/')
  async list() {
    return { users: [] };            // auto 200 + JSON
  }

  @Get('/{id}')
  async getOne(event: APIGatewayEvent) {
    const id = event.pathParameters?.id;
    return FusionResponse.ok({ id });
  }

  @Post('/')
  async create(event: APIGatewayEvent) {
    const body = JSON.parse(event.body || '{}');
    return FusionResponse.created(body).header('Location', `/users/${body.id}`);
  }
}
```

### Rules

- A class must be decorated with `@Controller(basePath)`.
- HTTP methods (`@Get`, `@Post`, etc.) take a path **relative to the
  controller's base**. Fusion concatenates them literally, so
  `@Controller('/users') + @Get('/{id}')` → final route `/users/{id}`.
- Method signatures: `(event: APIGatewayEvent) => any | Promise<any>`.
  Return a `FusionResponse` for fine control, or a plain value for auto
  JSON, or `undefined` for 204.
- You can have multiple `@Controller('/users')` classes (one for reads,
  one for writes, etc.). Fusion tags each with a unique internal id so
  they coexist. Only a true conflict (same HTTP method + same final
  path from two controllers) logs a warning and last-wins.

---

## 4. Use cases (`@UseCase` + `UCExecutor`)

Use cases encapsulate business logic and are executed, never injected:

```ts
import { UseCase, UC, UCExecutor } from '@fusion-framework/server';

@UseCase()
export class CreateUserUC extends UC {
  async execute(input: { name: string; email: string }) {
    // business logic here
    return { id: 'u-1', ...input };
  }
}

@Controller('/users')
export class UsersController {
  constructor(private ucExecutor: UCExecutor) {}

  @Post('/')
  async create(event: APIGatewayEvent) {
    const body = JSON.parse(event.body || '{}');
    const user = await this.ucExecutor.execute(CreateUserUC, body);
    return FusionResponse.created(user);
  }
}
```

**Anti-patterns:**

- `constructor(private createUserUC: CreateUserUC)` — wrong.
- `@Injectable()` on a use case — wrong, use `@UseCase()`.
- Extending something other than `UC` — wrong.

---

## 5. FusionResponse

The response builder. Chainable. All methods except getters return `this`.

```ts
FusionResponse.ok(data)                 // 200
FusionResponse.created(data)            // 201
FusionResponse.accepted(data)           // 202
FusionResponse.noContent()              // 204
FusionResponse.badRequest('why')        // 400
FusionResponse.unauthorized()           // 401
FusionResponse.forbidden()              // 403
FusionResponse.notFound()               // 404
FusionResponse.conflict()               // 409
FusionResponse.internalServerError()    // 500

new FusionResponse(data)
  .status(201)
  .header('X-Trace-Id', id)
  .setHeaders({ 'X-A': '1', 'X-B': '2' })
  .cache(300)                           // Cache-Control: max-age=300
  .noCache()
  .cors(['https://app.example'], ['GET', 'POST']);

// Content-type helpers (mutually exclusive with body)
.json(obj)                              // application/json
.text('hi')                             // text/plain
.html('<p/>')                           // text/html

// Binary (base64-encoded bodies)
FusionResponse.pdf(base64Pdf)
FusionResponse.image(base64Png, 'png')
FusionResponse.file(base64, 'application/zip', 'archive.zip')
new FusionResponse(base64Data).base64().header('Content-Type', 'application/octet-stream');
```

**`toResponse()` behavior** (what the Lambda actually returns):

- `null`/`undefined` body → empty string body.
- `string` body → passed through as-is; Content-Type is NOT
  auto-assigned (use `.header()`, `.binary()`, or a helper).
- Any other body → `JSON.stringify`'d, Content-Type defaulted to
  `application/json` if none set.
- `isBase64Encoded: true` is included iff `.base64()` was called.

---

## 6. Exceptions

All Fusion exceptions extend `FusionException` which carries `code`
(HTTP status) and `message`. They are `instanceof FusionException` and
`instanceof Error` after the ES5 prototype fix shipped in the framework.

```ts
import {
  ValidationException,        // 400
  UnauthorizedException,      // 401
  ForbiddenException,         // 403
  ResourceNotFoundException,  // 404
  ConflictException,          // 409
  UnprocessableEntityException,   // 422
  TooManyRequestsException,   // 429
  InternalServerErrorException,   // 500
  BadGatewayException,        // 502
  ServiceUnavailableException,    // 503
  FusionException,            // base — extend for custom codes
} from '@fusion-framework/server';

throw new ValidationException('email is required');
```

Uncaught throws inside a handler bubble up; `handleController` catches
them and returns `{ statusCode: error.code || 500, body: {message, requestId} }`.

### Custom exceptions

```ts
export class PaymentRequiredException extends FusionException {
  constructor(message = 'Payment Required') { super(402, message); }
}
```

---

## 7. Permissions (`@RequirePermission`)

### Taxonomy

Permissions are colon-separated tokens: `resource:action:scope`. A
wildcard `*` matches any segment. Examples:

- `operations:read:own` — read own operations
- `operations:*:area` — any action on operations in caller's area
- `*` — everything

Matching uses `hasPermission(granted, required)`:

- Exact match wins.
- `'*'` alone in `granted` matches anything.
- Per-segment wildcards match one segment each; segment counts must
  match (`a:b` does NOT match `a:b:c`).

### Enforcing

```ts
import { RequirePermission, getAuthContext } from '@fusion-framework/server';

@Controller('/operations')
export class OperationsController {
  @Get('/{id}')
  @RequirePermission('operations:read:own')   // all mode (default)
  async getOne(event: APIGatewayEvent) {
    const auth = getAuthContext(event);        // attached by decorator
    return FusionResponse.ok({ id: event.pathParameters!.id, userId: auth?.userId });
  }

  @Post('/{id}/assign')
  @RequirePermission('admin:*', 'operations:assign:area', { mode: 'any' })
  async assign(event: APIGatewayEvent) { /* ... */ }

  @Delete('/{id}')
  @RequirePermission('operations:delete:*', {
    onDenied: ({ required }) =>
      new FusionResponse({ code: 'NO_PERM', required }).status(403),
  })
  async remove(event: APIGatewayEvent) { /* ... */ }
}
```

- `mode: 'all'` (default) requires every listed permission.
- `mode: 'any'` requires at least one.
- `onDenied` customizes the 403 payload.
- Denied responses are a `FusionResponse` with status 403 and body
  `{ message: 'Forbidden', required, mode }` by default.

### Where permissions come from — `IAuthContextResolver`

The decorator does **not** hardcode where to read permissions from. It
delegates to a resolver registered under the `AUTH_CONTEXT_RESOLVER`
token. If none is registered, a default Cognito resolver reads
`event.requestContext.authorizer.claims` (or `.jwt.claims`).

Each project should register its own resolver during bootstrap:

```ts
import {
  container,
  AUTH_CONTEXT_RESOLVER,
  IAuthContextResolver,
  AuthContext,
} from '@fusion-framework/server';

class MyCustomAuthResolver implements IAuthContextResolver {
  resolve(event): AuthContext {
    // Custom Lambda authorizer stores CSV permissions in context.permissions
    const auth = (event.requestContext as any)?.authorizer ?? {};
    return {
      userId: auth.userId,
      permissions: (auth.permissions ?? '').split(',').filter(Boolean),
      roles: JSON.parse(auth.roles ?? '[]'),
      areas: JSON.parse(auth.areas ?? '[]'),
    };
  }
}

container.register(AUTH_CONTEXT_RESOLVER, { useClass: MyCustomAuthResolver });
```

Register BEFORE calling `app.createHandler(...)`. Resolver can be
async; the decorator awaits it.

### Reading the auth context in a handler

```ts
import { getAuthContext } from '@fusion-framework/server';

async handle(event: APIGatewayEvent) {
  const auth = getAuthContext(event);
  // auth is AuthContext | undefined
  // Only defined if @RequirePermission or @ResolveAuth ran on this method.
}
```

### Optional auth — `@ResolveAuth()`

For endpoints that allow anonymous access but want the auth context if
present:

```ts
@Get('/public-feed')
@ResolveAuth()
async feed(event: APIGatewayEvent) {
  const auth = getAuthContext(event);
  return auth ? personalizedFeed(auth.userId) : publicFeed();
}
```

---

## 8. Event listeners (non-HTTP)

For SQS, S3, EventBridge, or any custom event you send to the same
Lambda:

```ts
import { Listener, EvtListener } from '@fusion-framework/server';

// Event-name based (O(1) dispatch)
@Listener('user.created')
export class UserCreatedListener implements EvtListener {
  async handle(evt: { event: 'user.created'; data: any }) {
    return { processed: true };
  }
}

// Pattern based (dispatched when evt matches all patterns)
@Listener({
  patterns: {
    'Records[0].eventSource': 'aws:sqs',
    'Records[0].eventSourceARN': /arn:aws:sqs:.*:my-queue$/,
  },
})
export class MyQueueListener implements EvtListener {
  async handle(evt) { /* ... */ }
}
```

Register in the handler:

```ts
export const handler = app.createHandler({
  controllers: [UsersController],
  listeners: [UserCreatedListener, MyQueueListener],
});
```

Dispatch order: event-name exact match first, then the first registered
pattern whose config matches. No match → error response.

---

## 9. Dependency injection

Fusion uses [tsyringe](https://github.com/microsoft/tsyringe). Key
points:

- `@Controller`, `@Injectable`, and `@UseCase` all register the class
  with tsyringe.
- `@Inject(token)` for explicit injection by token.
- `@Stage()` is a param decorator that injects the deployment stage
  (`'dev'|'qa'|'staging'|'prod'`), inferred from the Lambda's invoked
  function ARN at request time.
- `container` is re-exported. Use `container.register(...)` in bootstrap
  to wire custom implementations (e.g. auth resolver, repositories).
- In tests, call `container.reset()` (full reset) or
  `container.clearInstances()` (keep registrations, drop singletons)
  between cases.

---

## 10. Bootstrap / handler

```ts
// handler.ts
import 'reflect-metadata';       // required at the very top
import { app, container, AUTH_CONTEXT_RESOLVER } from '@fusion-framework/server';
import { UsersController } from './controllers/users.controller';
import { MyCustomAuthResolver } from './auth/resolver';

container.register(AUTH_CONTEXT_RESOLVER, { useClass: MyCustomAuthResolver });

export const handler = app.createHandler({
  controllers: [UsersController],
  cors: {
    enabled: true,
    allowOrigins: ['https://app.example.com'],
    allowMethods: ['GET', 'POST', 'PUT', 'DELETE', 'PATCH'],
    allowHeaders: ['Content-Type', 'Authorization'],
    credentials: true,
    maxAge: 600,
  },
});
```

CORS config is optional. When enabled, headers are merged onto every
response (including error responses), with origin echo if in the
`allowOrigins` list.

---

## 11. Common pitfalls (and the right fix)

| Symptom | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| `Unregistered controller for route GET /users/{id}` | Path uses `:id` instead of `{id}` | Change decorator to `{id}` |
| `Method 'foo' not found in controller` (after collision fix: no longer possible) | Two `@Controller('/x')` used to collide | Already fixed — if you see this, file a bug |
| `[FusionServer Warning] Route conflict: GET /users/{id}` | Two distinct classes register the same method+path | Remove the duplicate or differentiate the path |
| `auth is undefined` inside handler | `@RequirePermission` / `@ResolveAuth` not applied, OR resolver not registered | Add the decorator; register resolver in bootstrap |
| Permissions always denied | Resolver returns `permissions: []`; usually because claims shape differs from default | Register a custom `IAuthContextResolver` |
| `instanceof FusionException` returns false | Pre-ES2015 target without prototype restoration | Already fixed in framework; re-run `npm install` |
| `FusionResponse` body is a string but arrives without Content-Type | Expected — string bodies don't auto-assign MIME | Use `.header('Content-Type', ...)`, `.text()`, `.html()`, or `.binary()` |
| Decorator complains in TS 5 (`2 args vs 3`) | Testing decorators with `@` syntax without `experimentalDecorators` | Apply decorators as functions in tests: `Get('/x')(Cls.prototype, 'm')` |

---

## 12. Testing patterns

```ts
import 'reflect-metadata';
import { container } from '@fusion-framework/server';

describe('UsersController', () => {
  afterEach(() => container.reset());

  it('uses injected repo', () => {
    const repo = { find: jest.fn().mockResolvedValue(null) };
    container.register('UserRepository', { useValue: repo });
    // ... resolve and test
  });
});
```

For testing `@RequirePermission` / `@ResolveAuth` without full server
bootstrap, apply the decorator manually (avoids TS 5 decorator
signature issues):

```ts
class Ctrl {
  async handle(evt: any) { return 'ok'; }
}
RequirePermission('ops:read:own')(Ctrl.prototype, 'handle');
```

Register a stub resolver:

```ts
container.register(AUTH_CONTEXT_RESOLVER, {
  useValue: { resolve: () => ({ permissions: ['ops:read:*'] }) },
});
```

---

## 13. Source map

- `src/fusion-server.ts` — main dispatcher
- `src/core/decorators/` — all decorators
  - `http/` — `@Get`, `@Post`, `@Put`, `@Patch`, `@Delete`
  - `interfaces/` — `@Controller`, `@Listener`
  - `dependencies/` — `@Injectable`, `@UseCase`, `@Inject`, `@Executor`
  - `stages/` — `@Stage()`
  - `security/` — `@RequirePermission`, `@ResolveAuth`, `getAuthContext`
- `src/core/classes/` — `FusionResponse`, `UC`, `UCExecutor`,
  `DefaultCognitoAuthResolver`
- `src/core/exceptions/` — exception hierarchy
- `src/core/interfaces/` — `EvtListener`, `ListenerMatchConfig`,
  `AuthContext` / `IAuthContextResolver`
- `src/core/utils/` — `pattern-matcher`, `permission-matcher`
- `src/core/registry.ts` — tsyringe helpers
- `test/` — Jest tests; double as usage examples

---

## 14. Versioning policy

- Additive (new decorators, new FusionResponse helpers): minor bump.
- Internal metadata format changes (e.g., the `controllerId|method`
  value format): patch bump; external code should not read raw
  `fusion:get`/`:post` metadata.
- Behavioral changes to HTTP response shape or route matching: major
  bump.
